results.title: [Literature Review] WeaveBench: A Long-Horizon, Real-World ...
results.content:
WeaveBench is a novel, long-horizon, real-world benchmark designed to evaluate computer-use agents (CUAs) operating with hybrid interfaces, specifically visual desktop control (GUI) and command-line/code execution (CLI/code). Existing benchmarks often assess these interfaces in isolation, failing to test an agent's ability to orchestrate complex workflows that necessitate interleaved operations across both modalities. WeaveBench addresses this critical gap by providing 114 tasks spanning 8 real-world work domains, each requiring agents to seamlessly combine GUI observations/actions with CLI/code operations within a single execution trajectory. Tasks are grounded in genuine user requests and publicly verifiable artifacts. [...] The benchmark's core methodology is built upon rigorous task admission criteria and a sophisticated evaluation protocol.
Task Admission Criteria: A WeaveBench task must satisfy three properties:

Task Construction: Tasks are built through a four-stage pipeline:

Evaluation Protocol: Trajectory-Aware Agentic Judge:
Traditional outcome-only grading is insufficient for hybrid rollouts, as final deliverables can be synthesized, metrics hard-coded, or states reached through specification-violating tool use. WeaveBench employs a trajectory-aware agentic judge (an isolated OpenClaw agent, fixed at GPT-5.5) that re-fetches evidence over multiple turns using file, image, and shell tools.
The scoring follows a five-layer pipeline: [...] Moonlight Logo
Moonlight Logo
Moonlight Logo

# [Literature Review] WeaveBench: A Long-Horizon, Real-World Benchmark for Computer-Use Agents with Hybrid Interfaces

results.title: [PDF] WEAVE: Unleashing and Benchmarking the In-context Interleaved
results.content:
WEAVEBench is a human-annotated benchmark of 100 items with 480 images, featuring a hybrid VLM judge eval-uation framework with four metrics that assess alignment with reference images, fidelity to original images, and cor-rectness of editing instructions. The benchmark evaluates models’ capabilities in multi-turn generation, visual memory, and world-knowledge reasoning across diverse domains, in-cluding science, creation, logic, and games. WEAVEBench reveals that current models struggle with in-context inter-leaved generation and exhibit performance degradation as content length increases, indicating substantial room for im-provement. [...] WEAVEBench is annotated by individuals with graduate-level STEM degrees. It comprises 100 items across 16 task categories, incorporating both multi-turn editing tasks re-quiring visual memory and challenges demanding world knowledge (e.g. cultural contexts, physical phenomena, and chemical processes). As illustrated in Figure 2, tasks include generating examples involving the Tokyo Tower and demon-strating comprehension of traffic signal reactions. All initial images are sourced from real-world data. During evaluation, the model generates outputs autonomously at each turn. For the error accumulation ablation (Section 4.1), we provide human-annotated references produced by SOTA models. [...] • We present WEAVEBench, the first human-annotated benchmark for interleaved multimodal comprehension and generation, featuring 100 carefully designed cases with 480 images and a hybrid VLM judge evaluation frame-work that assesses multi-turn generation, visual memory, and world-knowledge reasoning.
• Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that training on WEAVE-100k significantly improves perfor-mance on established benchmarks and facilitates the emer-gence of visual memory capabilities, while evaluation on WEAVEBench reveals persistent limitations in multi-turn, context-aware generation.

results.title: WeaveBench: Testing Hybrid Computer-Use Agents - YouTube
results.content:
In this AI Research Roundup episode, Alex discusses the paper: 'WeaveBench: A Long-Horizon, Real-World Benchmark for Computer-Use Agents with Hybrid Interfaces' WeaveBench is a new long-horizon benchmark designed to evaluate computer-use agents across hybrid interfaces, bridging graphical user interfaces (GUIs) and command-line interfaces (CLIs). Traditional evaluations often isolate these capabilities, allowing agents to bypass GUI requirements through programmatic shortcuts. WeaveBench addresses this with 114 complex tasks across 8 real-world domains like DevOps and CAD, requiring deep coordination and state tracking between tools. Additionally, the authors developed a trajectory-aware agentic judge that inspects workspace states to prevent shortcut behaviors and reward hacking. This [...] to prevent shortcut behaviors and reward hacking. This benchmark provides a highly realistic environment to test how effectively multimodal models can navigate real-world operating systems. results.content:
WeaveBench evaluates 4 core metrics:

| Metric | Code | Description | Requires Text | Requires Image |
 ---  --- 
| Key Point Correctness | KP | Measures whether the edited image satisfies the specified editing requirements. | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Visual Consistency | VC | Ensures non-target elements remain unchanged and maintains consistency with the original image. | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Image Quality | IQ | Evaluates the overall quality of the generated image. | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Accuracy | ACC | Measures the correctness of the reasoning result for comprehension tasks. | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

Consider adding your results to our leaderboard at Weave Project Page. Please contact weichow@qq.com for submission details!

results.title: WeaveBench | Long-Horizon Hybrid-Interface Benchmark for ...
results.content:
Deployed CUA runtimes already integrate GUI control, CLI/code execution, browsers, and external tools inside one agent loop. Existing benchmarks test these channels in isolation, leaving the orchestration layer under-measured. WeaveBench is built around tasks where success requires the agent to weave both channels together.

### GUI and CLI are complementary, not interchangeable.

GUIs expose rendered, transient, spatial state — canvases, dialogs, visual feedback. CLI/code expose structured, scriptable, persistent state — source files, configs, logs, services. Real workflows braid them: observe in the GUI, verify in the shell, edit in code, re-render, re-observe.

Real Ubuntu desktop 114 sandboxed tasks 8 work domains 4 deployed harnesses Trajectory-aware judge [...] WeaveBench 
 Release v0.1 · 114 tasks

A long-horizon, real-world benchmark for computer-use agents with hybrid interfaces

# WeaveBench

114 tasks across 8 real-world work domains, each requiring an agent to weave GUI observation with CLI/code execution within a single trajectory. Rollouts run inside a real Ubuntu sandbox; a trajectory-aware Agent-as-a-Judge audits eight quality dimensions and zeros credit on any of nine shortcut patterns (synthetic screenshots, hard-coded metrics, mock services…).

Core Idea Pipeline Results  Code Repo   arXiv:2606.09426   🤗 Dataset

 One-command setup bash scripts/setup.sh Installs deps + downloads dataset + 28 GB qcow2.  →

Figure 1 from the paper

results.title: Benchmarking Guide | Arweave Docs
results.content:
20,000 H1 hashes (50 \ 400)

20,000 H2 hashes (50 \ 400)

The `hash` benchmark reports

`hash`

H0: 1.5 ms

H1/H2: 0.2 ms

Each VDF step you will need 8,075 ms of compute time (40,000 \ 0.2 + 50 \ 1.5)

Implication is that you will need more than 8-cores to mine a 1 second VDF.

#### 1.1.2 Example 2

The full weave is 50 partitions

You are mining 20 partitions

Each VDF step you will compute

20 H0 hashes

8,000 H1 hashes (20 \ 400)

3,200 H2 hashes (20 \ (20/50) \ 400)

The `hash` benchmark reports

`hash`

H0: 1 ms

H1/H2: 0.1 ms

Each VDF step you will need 1,140 ms of compute time (11,200 \ 0.1 + 20 \ 1)

Implication is that you will need more than 1 core to mine a 1 second VDF.

### 1.2 Options

Usage: `./bin/arweave benchmark hash [options]` Options: [...] For the following examples, please keep in mind that with all the benchmarks, these computations should be taken as guides. In practice your hashing speed will be impacted by a number of factors not captured by the benchmark (e.g. contention with other processes running, impact of hyperthreading/SMT, etc...). Also you should budget your CPU capacity to exceed these calculations in order to accommodate all the other work your miner is doing (both within the Arweave node and at the system level).

For more information about how hashrate is calculated, please see Overview - Hashrate.

### 1.1 Examples

#### 1.1.1 Example 1

The full weave is 50 partitions

You are mining all 50 partitions

Each VDF step you will compute

50 H0 hashes

20,000 H1 hashes (50 \ 400)

20,000 H2 hashes (50 \ 400) [...] `hash`

The times reported are for a single thread (i.e. single core) of execution. The number of H0 or H1/H2 hashes your system can compute every second of wallclock time can be scaled up by the number of cores in your CPU. We don't yet have guidance on the impact of hyperthreading/SMT, so for now best to only count the number of physical cores on your CPU rather than virtual cores.

Some points to keep in mind. For each VDF step your miner will compute:

1 H0 hash for every partition

400 H1 hashes for every partition

0-400 H2 hashes for every partition

The specific number of H2 hashes is determined by how much of the weave you're mining.
